elements.sent-2411-501: In this work, we build a new Galactic extinction map at high Galactic latitude covering 5,577/4,555\,deg$^2$ ($13.5\%/11.0\%$ of the sky fraction) in the NGC/SGC.
elements.sent-2411-502: Our extinction map is constructed with tracers of gas in neutral, ionized, and molecular phases.
elements.sent-2411-503: Utilizing \ion{H {1 spectral maps, we adopt a data-driven approach to identify neutral gas in PPV space, segmenting it into distinct clouds to serve as fitting templates.}}
elements.sent-2411-504: This method is an improvement over previous \ion{H {1 -based dust maps, which did not incorporate multi-phase information and relied solely on a simple velocity cut to define gas clouds.}}
elements.sent-2411-505: We assess the performance of our template by examining the residuals from fitting it to the Planck maps at 353, 545, and 857\,GHz.
elements.sent-2411-506: Additionally, we produce a reddening map by fitting our templates to the SFD reddening map.
elements.sent-2411-507: Despite the enhanced analysis compared to previous \ion{H {1 -based dust templates, we observe only a modest improvement in reducing the residuals when fitting to the Planck maps.}}
elements.sent-2411-508: We further investigate the origin of the remaining large-scale residuals using ancillary data that traces variations in different dust-to-gas properties in the ISM.
elements.sent-2411-509: The principal conclusions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-510: At high-latitude regions, including the \ion{H {2 template in addition to \ion{H {1 reduces the residuals when fitting to Planck maps in some areas, but the improvement is modest.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-511: Currently available H$_2$ tracers built from CO emission lack the sensitivity needed to detect high-latitude molecular gas.
elements.sent-2411-512: Consequently, including the H$_2$ template does not help reduce the fitting residuals.
elements.sent-2411-513: Our new \ion{H {1 templates, constructed using a clustering algorithm, improve upon previous templates that were based on a simple velocity cut.}}
elements.sent-2411-514: The improvements are particularly notable in reducing sightlines that contain the Magellanic Stream, IVCs, or have a significant fraction of \ion{H {2 , which exhibit large positive or negative residuals when using previous \ion{H {1 -based dust templates.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-515: We find that most of the large-scale patterns in the residuals observed with previous \ion{H {1 -based templates cannot be effectively removed by these improved templates.}}
elements.sent-2411-516: We find evidence that the remaining residuals in some regions are associated with variations in the dust-to-gas ratio, dust emissivity, temperature, and magnetic field orientation.
elements.sent-2411-517: However, none of these factors appear to be the dominant effect responsible for the bulk of the residuals observed in our fit.
elements.sent-2411-518: We derive an upper bound on the dust temperature variation of $\sigma_T<1.28$~K in our high-latitude fields by assuming the residuals are entirely contributed by temperature variation and adopting a conservative mean temperature of $\overline{T =25$~K.}
elements.sent-2411-519: Our limit is lower than many previous dust temperature maps, suggesting that systematics may exist in their estimations.
elements.sent-2411-520: We detect the CIB in the residuals of fitting our template to the Planck map through cross-correlation with the CMB lensing map.
elements.sent-2411-521: This is expected, as our gas templates only trace the Galactic component.
elements.sent-2411-522: However, the CIB fluctuations are confined to much smaller scales, whereas the large-scale patterns in our residuals span over tens of degrees cannot be explained by CIB fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-523: There are a few potential future directions for improving dust templates.

elements.sent-2411-527: A joint analysis using multiple tracers to simultaneously constrain variations in dust properties could significantly enhance our existing analysis.

elements.sent-2411-669: Therefore, in total, we have five attributes in our clustering task for HI4PI data ($N_{\rm attr =5$) — spatial coordinates $x$, $y$, and $z$, velocity $v$, and the \ion{H {1 channel density $\Delta N_{\rm H\text{\sc I ^{\rm ch /\Delta v^{\rm ch}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-670: We normalize each attribute by subtracting its mean and dividing by its standard deviation.
elements.sent-2411-671: In the following, $\tilde{x}$, $\tilde{y}$, $\tilde{z}$, $\tilde{v}$, and $\tilde{\rho}$ denote the five normalized attributes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-672: The k-means clustering algorithm seeks for optimal clustering assignment based on Euclidean distance $d_{ij}$ between data point $i$ and cluster center $j$ of the input attribute: \begin{equation}\begin{split} d_{ij}^2 = (\tilde{x}_i-\tilde{x}_i)^2 + (\tilde{y}_i-\tilde{y}_i)^2 + (\tilde{z}_i-\tilde{z}_i)^2 + (\tilde{v}_i-\tilde{v}_i)^2 +(\tilde{\rho}_i-\tilde{\rho}_i)^2. \end{split}\end{equation} We can apply a weighting to each attribute.
elements.sent-2411-673: With the weighting on attributes, the distance metric is defined as \begin{equation}\begin{split} d_{ij}^2 = w^2_x(\tilde{x}_i-\tilde{x}_i)^2 + w^2_y(\tilde{y}_i-\tilde{y}_i)^2 + w^2_z(\tilde{z}_i-\tilde{z}_i)^2 + w^2_v(\tilde{v}_i-\tilde{v}_i)^2 +w^2_\rho(\tilde{\rho}_i-\tilde{\rho}_i)^2. \end{split}\end{equation} By symmetry, the weights are always set to the same value for $x$, $y$, and $z$.
elements.sent-2411-674: Therefore, without loss of generality, we set $w_x = w_y = w_z$ and explore the optimal values of $w_v$ and $w_\rho$ that provide the best fit to the data.
elements.sent-2411-675: In our implementation, during both optimization for the number of clusters $k$ and our final model with the selected $k$, we search for the best attribute weighting ($w_v$ and $w_\rho$) that minimizes the $\chi^2$ when fitting to Planck data.
elements.sent-2411-676: For our final choice of number of clusters (3/5 clusters for NGC/SGC), the optimal weights are $w_v = 3.56$ and $w_\rho = 0.022$.
elements.sent-2411-677: This implies that the velocity of clouds is the most crucial information in classifying \ion{H {1 into clusters, as expected, while the density of each 3D voxel is the least important feature.}}
elements.sent-2411-678: We implement the k-means clustering algorithm with the cluster.KMeans class in the scikit-learn package \citep{2011JMLR...12.2825P .}
elements.sent-2411-679: We use the default Lloyd’s method of k-means implementation, and set the relative tolerance parameter $tol=0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-680: We use their ``greedy k-means$++$'' method for setting initial cluster centroids, and set the $n\_init$ parameter to 1, meaning that we only run the clustering once instead of multiple times with different initializing and pick the one with the best performance.
